Idiomatic Expressions

  • 新硎初试 - Metaphor meaning 'a newly sharpened knife,' symbolizing a fresh start or initial attempt.
  • 游刃未能 - Phrase from *Zhuangzi* meaning 'the blade does not yet reach the ease of cutting,' indicating a lack of skill or experience.
  • 我与君皆有所司 - Formal expression meaning 'both I and you have duties to attend to.'
  • 何暇及此 - Rhetorical question meaning 'how can we spare time for this?'
  • 令上官闻之，弹章将不远矣 - Warning that if superiors hear about it, they will soon send a reprimand.
  • 予固谓君不欲暇也 - Statement meaning 'I firmly believe you do not wish to have time for this.'
  • 事果有碍 - Phrase meaning 'if there is indeed an obstacle.'
  • 予岂敢以孟浪贻君忧 - Self-deprecating statement meaning 'how dare I cause you trouble with recklessness?'
  • 然则君将奈何 - Rhetorical question meaning 'then what should you do?'
  • 君无漏言 - Imperative meaning 'do not let a word slip out.'
  • 翌日仍戒舆马，饬吏役，起居如常 - Command meaning 'the next day, prepare the carriage and horses, instruct the officials, and proceed as usual.'
  • 予自能偕君一往 - Statement meaning 'I will accompany you myself.'
  • 惝恍不定 - Phrase meaning 'in a state of confusion or uncertainty.'
  • 姑诺之 - Polite acceptance meaning 'I will agree for now.'
  • 甫至湖干 - Phrase meaning 'as soon as we arrived at the lakeside.'
  • 果有大游船舣以相待 - Statement meaning 'indeed, a large pleasure boat was waiting for us.'
  • 忽舆侧有人启曰 - Phrase meaning 'suddenly, someone at the side of the carriage spoke up.'
  • 白云叟先生敬候 - Respectful address meaning 'Mr. Baiyun Elder respectfully awaits.'
  • 不自由身已降舆 - Phrase meaning 'without realizing, I had already stepped down from the carriage.'
  • 驺从十数，拥一小犊车，驱迓道左 - Description meaning 'a dozen attendants, escorting a small ox-drawn carriage, met us on the side of the road.'
  • 执事甚恭，亟引卢登之 - Statement meaning 'the attendants were very respectful and quickly guided me onto the carriage.'
  • 癷然遂发，疾如风雨 - Phrase meaning 'the carriage suddenly set off, swift as wind and rain.'
  • 不转瞬即出钱塘城门 - Statement meaning 'in the blink of an eye, we were out of the Qiantang city gate.'
  • 卢心窃讶焉 - Phrase meaning 'Lu was secretly surprised.'
  • 自计来竣公事，即作嬉游 - Statement meaning 'he thought to himself that after finishing official business, he would go on a leisurely trip.'
  • 益非善策 - Phrase meaning 'this was even less of a good plan.'
  • 然已无如之何 - Statement meaning 'but there was nothing he could do about it.'

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '西湖已有东道主，不再司君刀笔矣' implies that someone else is now in charge of managing the affairs at West Lake, and the speaker (卢) is no longer handling official duties.
  • The clause '真君召去，代纪钱塘县事' indicates that the deputy county magistrate was called away by the True Lord and replaced by someone else to manage the affairs of Qiantang County.
  • The clause '真君嘱予勿言，言将有祸' suggests that the deputy county magistrate was instructed by the True Lord not to speak about the matter, as doing so would bring misfortune.
  • The clause '君之越俎以代者，实我也' reveals that the speaker (卢) was the one who replaced the deputy county magistrate in his duties.
  • The clause '不识为叟捉刀者' indicates that the deputy county magistrate did not know who the old man (叟) was writing for, suggesting a mystery or an unknown identity.

Proper Nouns

  • 卢 - The name of the main character, likely a high-ranking official.
  • 叟 - The name of the old man who helped the deputy county magistrate.
  • 西湖 - The name of the famous lake in Hangzhou.
  • 钱塘县 - The name of the county where the deputy county magistrate was managing affairs.
  • 铜章 - The seal of office, a symbol of authority.

  • 仙言所谓佳境者 - '仙言' means 'words of the immortals' or 'mythical advice,' and '佳境' means 'beautiful place' or 'ideal setting.' The phrase refers to the character's previous belief that this place is a paradise.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The door was pushed open, and wine and food were given to him. She smiled and said, 'Madam was worried that you would starve, and couldn't bear the punishment, so she gave you this to satisfy your hunger. You can eat.' After eating, the maid took the leftovers away. One-ming still lay naked, thinking of his father, and secretly shed tears. Later, after three drums had struck, he heard the maids talking, 'Madam can't hold her liquor anymore, and she wants to sleep. You can go to bed first.' They then led One-ming out of the dark room. The boudoir was filled with the scent of fragrant herbs, and the silver candles shone brightly. The embroidered curtains and brocade quilts were so beautiful that they couldn't be described. The group led One-ming to the bed and said, 'Great good fortune, lie flat here, is it not better than sleeping in a desolate pavilion?' One-ming lay down and felt the fragrant and soft bedding, which made him feel relaxed. After some time, a pair of gauze candles appeared, guiding a beautiful woman into the room. She said to herself, 'Foolish old man is not ashamed, persistently纠缠 with me and almost ruined my good thing.' She then asked the maid, 'Where is the naked child?' The maid replied, 'He is already in the bedding.'

  • 惟判令诸商尽收翁之遗产，而以诸姬母子悉归一鸣 - Phrase meaning 'only ordered the merchants to fully collect the old man's inheritance, and to give all the ladies and their children to One Ming.'
  • 翁族无敢违，一鸣遂携眷侨寓淮上 - Phrase meaning 'the old man's family dared not disobey, One Ming then took his family and settled in the Huai region.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 直指 - Title for an imperial censor or inspector.
  • 议礼 - Refers to discussions about ritual and ceremonial matters, which were highly sensitive in imperial China.
  • 两淮盐道 - The salt commissioner of the Two Huai regions, a significant administrative post.
  • 盐道 - A high-ranking official responsible for salt production and distribution.
  • 翁之族 - Refers to the old man's family, emphasizing their social status as merchants.
  • 一鸣 - The name of the protagonist, possibly a play on words (一鸣惊人, 'One Ming astonishes everyone').
  • 诸姬 - Refers to the other ladies or concubines in the old man's household.
  • 御史 - A title for a censor or inspector, often with judicial powers.
  • 治哑 - Refers to the ability to treat or cure muteness, a specialized skill.

  • 直指 - The title of the official.
  • 两淮 - The Two Huai regions, a significant administrative area.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 頓覺欲言，即言矣 - Phrase indicating that the person suddenly felt the urge to speak and did so immediately; '頓覺' means 'suddenly felt,' and '即言矣' means 'and spoke immediately.'

  • 及笄，猶未字也 - Phrase indicating that the daughter has reached the age of marriage but is still unmarried; '及笄' means 'reached the age of marriage,' and '猶未字也' means 'still unmarried.'
  • 曾作詠燕詩曰 - Phrase indicating that the daughter once wrote a poem about swallows; '詠燕' means 'poem about swallows.'
  • 非向金閨惜羽毛，雙飛只慮近蓬蒿 - Line from the poem indicating that the swallows do not care about their feathers in the golden chambers but worry about flying close to the thorny grass; '金閨' means 'golden chambers,' and '蓬蒿' means 'thorny grass.'
  • 雪衣籠內終嫌媚，霜爪風前亦憚勞 - Line from the poem indicating that the swallows are beautiful in the snow-white cage but are also tired of the wind and cold; '雪衣' means 'snow-white,' and '憚勞' means 'tired of labor.'
  • 吟至此苦思不就 - Phrase indicating that the daughter struggled to complete the poem; '苦思不就' means 'struggled to complete.'
  • 因自矢曰 - Phrase indicating that the daughter made a vow; '自矢' means 'made a vow.'
  • 有能續成者，吾即事之 - Phrase indicating that anyone who can complete the poem will marry her; '續成' means 'complete,' and '即事之' means 'will marry.'
  • 直指取其詩以示紳士 - Phrase indicating that the official showed the poem to the scholars; '紳士' refers to scholars or gentlemen.
  • 踵之者凡數十人，悉揮去 - Phrase indicating that many people tried to continue the poem but were all dismissed; '踵之者' means 'those who followed,' and '悉揮去' means 'all dismissed.'
  • 後聞一鳴能詩，召使續之 - Phrase indicating that the official later heard that one person could write poetry and summoned them to complete the poem; '一鳴' refers to the person who could write poetry.
  • 意固有在也 - Phrase indicating that the official had a specific intention; '意固有在也' means 'had a specific intention.'
  • 一鳴援筆立成曰 - Phrase indicating that the person took up the brush and completed the poem immediately; '援筆' means 'took up the brush,' and '立成' means 'completed immediately.'
  • 落月屋梁眠自穩，飛花簾幕舞偏高 - Line from the poem indicating that the moon sets on the beam and the person sleeps peacefully, while the flying flowers dance high on the curtain; '落月' means 'setting moon,' and '飛花' means 'flying flowers.'
  • 女覽之喜曰 - Phrase indicating that the daughter was pleased upon reading the poem; '覽之' means 'read it,' and '喜曰' means 'said happily.'

Syntactic Ambiguities

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 外史氏曰 - Conventional opening indicating the author or commentator is addressing the reader directly.
  • 人之英华多因言泄 - Literally means 'the brilliance of a person often leaks through words,' referring to the idea that a person's inner qualities can be revealed through speech.
  • 哑则克全其天 - 'If one is mute, one can preserve one's natural state.' This suggests that not speaking can help maintain one's original nature.
  • 声色当前，真元剥蚀 - 'When sounds and colors are present, the true essence is eroded.' This implies that external stimuli can diminish one's inner purity.
  • 一鸣惊人 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'to make a great impression with a single action' or 'to achieve sudden fame.'
  • 不若不鸣之为愈也 - 'It is better not to speak at all.' This suggests that silence is preferable to sudden fame.
  • 守口如瓶 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'to keep one's mouth closed like a bottle,' emphasizing the importance of silence.
  • 徒贻笑口 - 'To be laughed at in vain.' This implies that one's efforts are futile if they lead to ridicule.
  • 不亦哀哉 - 'Is it not sad?' This rhetorical question expresses sorrow or pity.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 外史氏 - A term often used in classical Chinese texts to refer to a historian or commentator, suggesting a reflective or didactic tone.
  • 随园老人 - Reference to Yuan Mei (袁枚), a famous Qing dynasty poet and scholar who used the pen name '随园老人' (Old Man of Sui Garden).
  • 蕊榜 - Refers to the imperial examination results, specifically the list of successful candidates.
  • 云逵 - Metaphorical expression referring to the path to success or high office, often used in the context of the imperial examinations.

  • 及酣，拔劍斫地，慷慨悲歌，旁若無人，人因以酒狂目之 - '及酣' means 'when drunk,' '拔劍斫地' means 'draws his sword and slashes the ground,' '慷慨悲歌' means 'sings a heroic and sorrowful song,' '旁若無人' means 'as if there were no one else around,' and '人因以酒狂目之' means 'people thus call him 'drunkenly wild.'' This describes the dramatic and unrestrained behavior of the person when drunk.

  • 叩棺而祝之曰 - '叩棺' means 'knocked on the coffin,' '而祝之' means 'and said to her,' '曰' means 'said.'
  • 鲰生不才，適虛琴瑟 - '鲰生' means 'a humble person,' '不才' means 'unworthy,' '適' means 'just now,' '虛琴瑟' means 'left the zither and lute empty.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 划然震响 - Describes a sudden, loud sound.

  • 不沃而自解 - Means 'without needing to be wiped, it naturally dissipates.'
  • 卧病床蓐 - Means 'to lie ill in bed.'
  • 不胜惊异，咋舌而返 - Means 'too shocked to say anything, they returned with their tongues hanging out.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 新婚得意 - Refers to the joy and satisfaction of a new marriage.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence structure is elliptical and requires careful parsing to understand the polite inquiry.
  • The metaphorical expression needs to be rendered in a way that captures both the literal and figurative meanings.
  • The sequence of actions (turning, wanting to return, falling) must be clearly separated in English.
  • The cause-and-effect relationship between the sweat dissipating and running must be clear.
  • The sentence structure is elliptical and requires careful parsing to understand the polite inquiry about the new marriage.

  • 每遇耕获，辄祝曰：“天盍佑我。” - Idiomatic expression meaning that every time he encountered farming or harvesting, he would pray, 'May heaven help me.'
  • 人遂谓之祝天翁 - Indicates that people began calling him 'Zhu Tian Weng,' a nickname based on his habit of praying to heaven.
  • 暮年鳏居，有一子，亦学稼 - Describes an elderly widower with a son who also learned farming.
  • 年已而立，犹未偶也 - Idiomatic expression meaning that although he was in his thirties, he was still unmarried.
  • 父子茕然，出作入息，比邻多怜之 - Describes the father and son living alone, working outside and resting inside, and being pitied by their neighbors.
  • 或劝曰：“翁之发短矣，为儿纳妇，不有人盄彼南亩耶？” - Someone advised, 'Your hair is thinning, so arrange a marriage for your son, won't it benefit him to have a wife in the fields?'
  • 翁笑曰：“天佑我，筋力尚健。俟予衰老，伊婚未迟也。” - The old man laughed and said, 'Heaven helps me, my strength is still good. Wait until I am old, it's not too late for him to marry.'
  • 闻者咸嗤其贪吝 - Those who heard this all laughed at his greed and stinginess.
  • 一日，翁他出，子独力作，忽闻丛莽中有人笑曰：“丈夫之髭出矣，犹未授室耶？盍援我，予为尔妇。” - One day, when the old man was away, the son worked alone and suddenly heard someone laughing in the thickets, saying, 'Your mustache has grown, but you are still unmarried? Why not help me, I will be your wife.'
  • 子惊顾，回野无人，因复耕 - The son was startled and looked around, but there was no one in the field, so he continued working.
  • 旋又闻声语曰：“不援我，将鳏此一世矣。” - Shortly after, he heard the voice again, saying, 'If you don't help me, you will remain single for life.'

[424]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 子亦伟男 - A phrase meaning 'You are also a great man,' used to praise the male character.
  • 遂相得甚欢 - Means 'they became very happy with each other,' indicating a positive emotional connection.
  • 事讫 - Means 'after the matter was done,' referring to the completion of a specific event.
  • 若言为吾妇，其信然乎 - A question asking if the woman truly intends to be his wife, with '信然' meaning 'indeed so.'
  • 业已妇矣，何问焉 - Means 'I am already your wife, why ask?' with '业已' meaning 'already' and '何问焉' meaning 'why ask?'
  • 不然 - Means 'no' or 'not so,' used to disagree with the previous statement.
  • 所谓妇者 - Means 'what is meant by being a wife,' introducing a definition.
  • 事吾亲，育吾子，操吾家计 - A list of duties: 'serve my parents, raise my children, manage my household affairs.'
  • 良不止为一夕之欢 - Means 'it is not just about one night's pleasure,' emphasizing the long-term commitment.
  • 此亦非大难事 - Means 'this is not a difficult thing,' indicating that the task is manageable.
  • 妾为父母所贱 - Means 'I was despised by my parents,' reflecting a negative past experience.
  • 殁后厝之浅土 - Means 'after death, I was buried in shallow soil,' with '厝' meaning 'to bury.'
  • 霜露沁骨，灌莽缠骸 - Describes the harsh conditions of the burial site, with '霜露' meaning 'frost and dew,' '沁骨' meaning 'penetrate the bones,' '灌莽' meaning 'overgrown vegetation,' and '缠骸' meaning 'cling to the bones.'
  • 更惧为豺犬所搰 - Means 'I fear being dug up by wild dogs,' with '搰' meaning 'to dig up.'
  • 徒饱馋口 - Means 'only to satisfy the hunger of beasts,' with '馋口' meaning 'hungry mouths.'
  • 君能徙妾高阜，且深埋之 - Means 'if you can move me to a high place and bury me deeply,' with '徙' meaning 'move,' '高阜' meaning 'high place,' and '深埋' meaning 'bury deeply.'
  • 当为君鬼妻 - Means 'I will be your ghostly wife,' with '鬼妻' meaning 'ghost wife.'
  • 一切如命 - Means 'everything as you command,' indicating full obedience.
  • 鬼亦能诞育如人耶 - A question asking if ghosts can give birth like humans, with '诞育' meaning 'give birth.'
  • 凡人暴卒者，魂气凝聚，不即散 - Means 'for those who die suddenly, their spirit gathers and does not disperse immediately,' with '暴卒' meaning 'die suddenly' and '魂气凝聚' meaning 'spirit gathers.'
  • 能施能受，妊娠如常 - Means 'they can give and receive, and conceive as usual,' with '能施能受' meaning 'can give and receive' and '妊娠如常' meaning 'conceive as usual.'
  • 病而后殁，则不能矣 - Means 'if they die after being ill, then they cannot,' with '病而后殁' meaning 'die after being ill.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 鬼妻 - A concept in Chinese folklore where a deceased person can become a ghostly spouse.
  • 暴卒 - Refers to sudden death, often associated with supernatural phenomena.
  • 魂气凝聚 - A belief in the gathering of spirit after death, which can influence the living.
  • 妊娠如常 - Reflects the idea that spirits can still engage in biological processes, a concept found in some Chinese ghost stories.
